data.title: Smurfit Westrock: Strong Fundamentals Despite Short-Term Challenges
data.description:
We're revisiting the Smurfit Westrock Plc (NYSE:SW) investment opportunity to provide further commentary and insights following the Q2 release. Our previous analysis explicitly stated, "The Smurfit Westrock merger presents strategic value at a discounted valuation, offering a compelling buy opportunity." We believe this company remains difficult to assess; however, it is important not to value it on a P/E basis due to its ongoing restructuring efforts.

Before the merger, we were covering both WestRock and Smurfit Kappa. Now we observe: 1) a valuation discount, 2) visible operational efficiencies with $400 million synergies, 3) the potential for peace between Russia and Ukraine that might reduce COGS, and 4) a global presence that helps mitigate risks from tariffs & counter-tariffs.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive Take

Smurfit WestRock reported Q2 2025 results in line with expectations on an adjusted basis. Top-line sales reached $7.94 billion, and turnover missed Wall Street analysts' estimates by 0.03%. By regions, North America showed substantial improvement, recording $752 million in adjusted EBITDA and a 15.8% margin, driven by early synergy realization. The EMEA and Asian regions remained stable, while LATAM posted a standout EBITDA margin of 23.7%. Overall, the company delivered an adjusted EBITDA of $1.21 billion (15.3% margin - Fig 2). Going down to the P&L reporting, a $26 million net loss was recorded due to $280 million in restructuring and impairment costs related to ongoing facility closures. Consensus was expecting a net profit of $320.04 million.

Looking at the corrugated volumes, Smurfit WestRock reported a decline in North America and LATAM, reflecting end-market softness. The company continued investing heavily in operational upgrades, including facility closures, that are expected to deliver long-term EBITDA and CAPEX savings.

Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

  1. While the results may not appear entirely positive, we firmly believe that Management's reaffirmation of the full-year Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $5.0-$5.2 billion (Fig. 3) and the declaration of a $0.43 dividend are strong indicators of confidence in the company's performance. Additionally, the company has already internalized ~$180 million of the targeted $400 million in synergies, aiming for ~$350 million by year-end;
  2. During the earnings call, the management reported a minimal tariffs impact, confirming our thesis. But more importantly, the CEO confirmed that 40% of North American corrugated plants moved from loss to profit over the first semester. 60% of loss-making corrugated facilities in the region are expected to profit within a year. This essential element mentioned during the earnings call could increase margins. This strategy of value-over-volume, while optimizing costs (through mill closures), will provide earnings suitability over the long term. The CEO also noted that many European paper mills are in "desperate trouble," and anticipated closures ahead. We are not surprised to see Germany in trouble. This is an opportunity for a structure player such as Smurfit WestRock to increase its market share penetration;
  3. Despite higher interest expense, Fitch upgraded the company's long-term issuer rating to BBB+ with a stable outlook (from BBB). Furthermore, the company has already set a plan in place to lower net EBITDA leverage to 2.0x;
  4. On the upside, consumer goods companies (Pepsi, Mondelez, etc.) increasingly shift toward smaller package sizes to retain inflation-weary consumers by offering lower-priced entry points across product lines. Smaller packs often generate higher profits per unit and positively impact players such as Smurfit Westrock. While each smaller unit uses less material, the overall increase in packaging SKUs and units sold will likely drive higher aggregate demand for paper-based packaging, particularly in the F&B and personal care segments.

Fig 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

While the stock price has declined since the Q2 update and headlines have been mixed, the company delivered a decent Q2. In our initiation of coverage, we anticipated an EBITDA of $5.5 billion (above the company's yearly guidance) thanks to a tailwind from US/EU corrugated box pricing increases. We were expecting higher guidance in H1, but this was not the case, so we believe additional integration costs exist. With a $5 billion EBITDA and no change with our target multiple (8x), our enterprise value reached $40 billion. This EBITDA target multiple is at a 20% discount on the Packaging Corp of America of a 10x EV/ EBITDA multiple. This valuation also aligns with the sector median FWD EV/EBITDA average (Fig. 4). With the current net debt, Smurfit WestRock equity value reached $27.2 billion, and we derive a target price of $51.48 per share. This implied a 14% upside, without the current 3.5% dividend yield. This is a base case scenario, and we do not consider demand recovery and full commercial improvement initiatives.

Fig 4

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) lower corrugated demand, 2) inventory management from clients, 3) integration risks and combined deal synergies, 4) change in COGS (energy price, chemicals, and pulp) combined with wage inflation, 5) FX changes, and 6) higher competition even if the market is progressing with a sector consolidation.

Conclusion

Although Smurfit Westrock reported negative profit, we believe this is due to temporary factors such as restructuring costs and higher interest expenses, which the company aims to resolve over time. At this price, the company remains attractive, and considering the several moving parts post-merger, we remain overweighted.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Magnolia Oil & Gas: Another Improvement To Its Guidance As It Approaches 100,000 BOEPD
data.description:
Magnolia Oil & Gas (NYSE:MGY) reported another strong quarter of production results, with Q2 2025 production around 1% above its guidance along with D&C capex below expectations. The continued production outperformance allowed Magnolia to raise its full-year total production guidance by another 2% with no change in its full-year capex budget.

Magnolia now expects 10% year-over-year total production growth, and I believe that it can also deliver around 4% to 5% oil production growth.

Magnolia also expects to benefit from reduced cash income taxes which may add up to around $30 million to $35 million per year in savings based on current 2025 and 2026 strip prices.

I project that Magnolia can generate approximately $250 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow and have increased its estimated value from $25 per share to $26 per share (at long-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas).

With Magnolia trading at around $24 per share though, I have moved back to a neutral outlook on it. Magnolia was just slightly undervalued enough for me to consider it a buy last time I looked at it.

Q2 2025 Results

Magnolia expected approximately 97,000 BOEPD in Q2 2025 production (roughly flat compared to Q1 2025 production) with $110 million in D&C capex. It ended up producing approximately 98,200 BOEPD with around $95 million in D&C capex. Magnolia's total production and oil production both increased by approximately 2% compared to Q1 2025.

Magnolia generated $107 million in free cash flow during the quarter, of which approximately $49 million went to repurchasing 2.2 million shares of common stock. Another $29 million went towards Magnolia's $0.15 per share quarterly dividend.

Magnolia also made a number of small acquisitions in late June and early July that added 18,000 net Giddings acres and around 500 BOEPD (approximately 35% oil) in production for a combined total of $40 million. These acquisitions did not impact Magnolia's Q2 2025 production.

Magnolia's lease operating expenses dropped by 10% per BOE from Q1 2025 to Q2 2025, helped by lowered workover expenses. While Magnolia expects a partial rebound in the second half of the year, it still expects its 2H 2025 lease operating expense to be several percent lower than Q1 2025 levels.

Notes On Guidance

Magnolia revised its total production guidance upward again, calling for +10% year-over-year production growth, up roughly 2% from its prior guidance and around 4% from its original 2025 guidance.

Magnolia has maintained its full-year capex budget at around $430 million to $470 million (which is around $25 million lower than its original guidance). The recent small acquisitions would add only around 0.3% to Magnolia's full-year 2025 production, so most of its guidance improvement is from continuing strong well productivity and capital efficiency.

2H 2025 Outlook

Magnolia may now end up with around 98,700 BOEPD in full-year 2025 production, including around 40,000 barrels per day of oil production.

This would mean that Magnolia would average around 100,000 BOEPD in 2H 2025 production, with around 40,500 barrels of oil production per day. This is roughly 2% production growth compared to Q2 2025 levels.

The current strip for the second half of 2025 is roughly $68 WTI oil and $3.40 Henry Hub natural gas now.

At those commodity prices, Magnolia may be able to generate around $689 million in 2H 2025 revenues.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 7,452,000 $66.00 $492
NGLs 5,152,000 $21.00 $108
Gas 34,776,000 $2.55 $89
Total Revenues $689

Magnolia expects its lease operating expense to end up around $5.25 per BOE during 2H 2025. It also expects minimal cash income taxes for 2025 and 2026 now (at current strip) with the impact of the new tax legislation. It paid $8 million in cash income taxes in 1H 2025 and I had previously modeled around $35 million in cash income taxes for the full-year, so this is incrementally positive for Magnolia.

$ Million
Lease Operating $97
Gathering, Transportation, and Processing $34
Taxes Other Than Income $40
Cash G&A $34
Net Cash Interest $8
Capex $225
Cash Income Taxes $0
Total $438

Magnolia is now projected to generate $251 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow, which would give it around $194 million to put towards share repurchases and minor acquisitions after paying its current $0.15 per share quarterly dividend.

Notes On Valuation

I have slightly increased my estimate of Magnolia's value from $25 per share to $26 per share based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas.

This increase is partly due to Magnolia's continued production outperformance, with its production guidance improving by a couple percent. I have also assumed that Magnolia's oil production ends up around 4% to 5% higher year-over-year (slightly above its original low-single digit guidance for oil production growth).

As well, Magnolia's cash income taxes might go from around $35 million per year (in 2025 and 2026) at the current strip to a negligible amount, so that in itself would add around 18 cents per share to Magnolia's annual free cash flow in the near term.

Conclusion

Magnolia continued to report strong production results, leading to another increase in its 2025 production guidance without increasing its capex budget. Magnolia is also benefiting from lower cash income tax expectations and may be able to generate around $250 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow at current strip prices.

Magnolia's capital efficiency and well outperformance (along with the income tax savings) results in its estimated value increasing from $25 per share to $26 per share. It is trading around 8% below that level, so I'd consider it marginally undervalued, but not quite enough to consider it a buy at its current price.

data.title: FDV: Active Dividend ETF With Performance Restrained By Low Beta
data.description:
Federated Hermes U.S. Strategic Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:FDV), an actively managed dividend-focused ETF leveraging the potential of the quality and low volatility factors, attracted my attention just about two and a half years ago.

And while I dedicated quite a few paragraphs of the first article to the discussion of why FDV is a decent product, I was not open to the idea of starting coverage with a Buy rating, as a too-short trading history always holds me back from being confidently bullish on an ETF.

However, when I was revisiting it in April of this year, this logic was no longer working, as its trading history had increased to about two years and five months, which was already long enough to prepare some relevant annualized data. Moreover, the most recent performance back then was speaking in favor of it, as the tariff uncertainty was reverberating through the markets, but FDV was not in the camp of those most afflicted, as its low beta exposure combined with its high quality secured some immunity to the chaos. However, I still warned that the exact same low beta exposure could detract more than contribute, and maintaining a large allocation to it means quite significant gains missed.

And this time, I am doing the exact same thing, with the main difference being that my point on low beta is now reinforced with more evidence: since the previous coverage, FDV has grossly underperformed the S&P 500, which it is benchmarked against.

So before we proceed to the discussion, which, as usual, will include a deep dive into performance and factor nuances, I should clarify that I maintain the Hold rating, which mostly reflects its strong quality exposure. However, I am not in the bullish cohort, and there is a diminutive chance that I will become bullish on FDV in the future. I see no reason why quality- and low volatility-oriented investors should favor it over the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD), which has a lower expense ratio, a higher dividend yield, and is more liquid. I will provide data to corroborate in the ensuing paragraphs.

What Is FDV's Strategy?

According to the Federated Hermes website, the ETF is managed actively, with the goal being twofold: income and capital appreciation, and "investing primarily in high dividend-paying U.S. stocks with dividend growth potential" is what is supposed to allow FDV to achieve these goals. But what stocks fall into that category? As described in the prospectus:

The Adviser defines high-dividend-paying stocks as those with a higher dividend yield than the S&P 500 Index’s average dividend yield.

Overall, the methodology is fundamentals-oriented:

The Adviser’s security selection process involves prioritizing stocks based on attractive combinations of dividend yield and dividend growth potential over time following review of appropriate fundamental criteria, including, but not limited to, balance sheet strength, earnings growth, and cash flow durability. Those companies that rank as highly attractive in the prioritization process are closely scrutinized for inclusion in the portfolio using bottom-up fundamental proprietary research.

FDV Performance: Disadvantages Of Low Beta Are Crystal Clear

Despite being an actively managed ETF, FDV is quite reluctant when it comes to adding and removing stocks. For instance, since my April article, Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) was ousted and Sysco Corporation (SYY) was added, and that's it. And to my pleasure, it makes returns attribution a much easier task compared to high-turnover ETFs.

So why has FDV underperformed the S&P 500 amid such buoyant conditions? My calculations show that 13 stocks that were in the red are the main detractors, chief among them being Clorox (CLX), which has declined by around 9.7% between April 18 and July 30. Overall, the consumer staples sector was one of the worst performing over this period, with the average price return of -2.29%. Utilities, FDV's favorite sector, were also performing sluggishly for the exact same reason as consumer staples: the defensive thesis lost its allure as the markets returned to greed. Financials lent some support but have not changed anything fundamentally.

Sector Weight in FDV as of July 30 Average price return
Communication 3.5% -3.9%
Consumer Staples 12.5% -2.3%
Materials 4.6% 0.3%
Industrials 5.4% 2.3%
Utilities 18.0% 2.7%
Real Estate 8.1% 2.8%
Consumer Discretionary 3.3% 7.8%
Health Care 15.1% 8.3%
Energy 9.2% 13.1%
Information Technology 4.4% 18.5%
Financials 14.3% 27.6%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF. Based on the share prices as of April 18 and July 30. Data for SYY and BMY was not included

And as I promised, below is the data to prove that low beta was the culprit.

Group Median 24M Beta
Stocks that were down over the period 0.24
Stocks that were up over the period 0.62
Stocks that rose by over 20% over the period (9 in total) 1.14

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF

However, I do not want my analysis to look one-sided, so a few words should be said in defense of FDV. The fact is, its YTD total return is quite close to that of IVV thanks to its January–March momentum, while SCHD is barely in the green chiefly because of the energy sector's volatility that has detracted a lot from its returns, with consumer staples (especially names like PepsiCo (PEP) and Target (TGT)) being the second main detractor.

Sector Weight in FDV Weight in SCHD
Utilities 18.02% 0.04%
Health Care 15.12% 15.79%
Financials 14.30% 9.08%
Consumer Staples 13.51% 18.84%
Energy 9.15% 20.02%
Real Estate 8.13% 0.00%
Industrials 5.38% 11.83%
Materials 4.58% 3.16%
Information Technology 4.39% 8.34%
Communication Services 3.53% 4.39%
Consumer Discretionary 3.32% 8.19%

Created by the author using data from the ETFs. Weights as of July 30

The SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500® High Dividend ETF (SPYD), which has methodological similarities with FDV, also performed much weaker, while the Franklin U.S. Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (LVHD) beat it slightly.

Also, I believe it is necessary to revisit its long-term performance, even though since April, with three full calendar months added, not much has changed.

We see that over December 2022–June 2025, FDV was incapable of at least matching the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF's (IVV) return, let alone exceeding it. The good news is that passively managed peers SCHD and LVHD did much worse.

FDV Portfolio: SCHD Is Still Mostly Ahead

My approach today is similar to one I opted for in April: juxtaposing FDV's factor story to those of SCHD and IVV. For clarity, IVV fits excellently in this comparison, as it tracks FDV's benchmark. And SCHD is selected mainly because it is one of the largest and most liquid dividend ETFs in the U.S. with an immense following (and an army of skeptics, too).

A necessary remark is that in the table above, EV/EBITDA was adjusted for IVV to eliminate the effect of figures over 100x on the final result.

So as of July 30, there were 51 equities in the FDV portfolio, most of them being value stocks from the S&P 500. Overall, we see a relatively high-dividend, high-quality, growth-light mix, with little change over the past few months. I like its value tilt that is in part illustrated by the portfolio's weighted average dividend yield of 3.75% (the dividend yield of the ETF itself is 2.94%), 3.2x that of IVV. But still, there is nothing particularly impressive here:

  • SCHD is mostly cheaper (its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher confirms that).
  • SCHD's holdings are of higher quality (Return on Assets, adjusted Return on Equity, and Return on Total Capital, etc., tell a lot).
  • Factor in SCHD's 6 bps expense ratio vs. FDV's 50 bps and the superior liquidity of the former, and a case for FDV becomes even more complicated to defend.

Final Thoughts

In sum, FDV is a low-beta dividend ETF that is doing what it is supposed to by design: delivering the yield that is well in excess of IVV's, performing a little better than the market during downturns, and then, inevitably, lagging it during recoveries.

So my conclusion today is that FDV has a few issues, but they are not serious enough to cross it off the dividend ETF watchlist and forget. This fund does deserve at least some investor attention. However, a Buy rating is excessive and unjustified.

data.title: Franklin Biotechnology Discovery Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Advisor Class: FTDZX Class A: FBDIX Commentary | as of June 30, 2025

Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equities collectively rose during 2025's second quarter (2Q25) after bouncing back from their April lows. As measured by the MSCI (MSCI) All Country World Index in US-dollar terms, nine out of the 11 global equity sectors advanced; information technology (IT), communication services and industrials produced the highest returns, while energy and health care retreated. In terms of investment style, global growth stocks significantly outperformed their value-oriented counterparts, regardless of company size. With health care stocks largely absent from the 2Q25 global uptrend, four out of six related industries sold off, with the steepest declines occurring in health care providers and services, life sciences tools and services, and biotechnology. Health care technology was an outlier to the upside, along with the health care equipment and supplies industry. Overall weakness in biotech was driven by a combination of US-specific policy uncertainties, rising operational costs, and financial pressures from Medicaid redeterminations. Biotech stocks, particularly smaller firms reliant on global supply chains for research and manufacturing, were impacted by the specter of increased costs and disrupted trade flows. The uncertainty around tariff policies raised concerns about higher costs for raw materials, equipment, and clinical trial supplies, which disproportionately affect capital-intensive biotech firms. Some investors likely moved away from health care due to these headwinds, along with the sector's defensive nature and slow-growth outlook, which were less appealing in a bullish market environment.

  • Contributors: Stock selection in the biotechnology industry and beneficial overweighting in health care services.

  • Detractors: Stock selection and an underweighting in the life sciences tools and services industry and, to a lesser extent, stock selection in pharmaceuticals.

  • Outlook: We believe the biotechnology industry is poised for long-term growth due to its competitive advantages, innovation, and increasing pharmaceutical expenditures driven by aging populations. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and novel therapies are expanding market opportunities, despite risks related to inflation, regulatory and reimbursement challenges (i.e., drug pricing reforms).

Performance Review

  • Within the biotech industry, the fund's slightly-negative return compared well with the index's larger overall losses, thanks to several overweighted or off-index contributors that rallied with double-digit percentage gains, led by Merus (MRUS), Insmed (INSM), ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), Ascendis Pharma (ASND), UniQure (QURE), CytomX Therapeutics (CTMX) (purchased during the period) and Akebia Therapeutics (AKBA). We also maintained lighter-than-index exposures to poor performers such as Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) and avoided numerous index component companies that sustained significant losses.

  • Among the key contributors cited above, CytomX, a clinical-stage oncology treatment developer, was a substantial outlier to the upside. CytomX announced a significant milestone with promising interim results from its ongoing Phase 1 dose-escalation study of CX-2051, an antibody-drug conjugate targeting EpCAMs (epithelial cell adhesion molecules) playing a crucial role in advanced colorectal cancer. CytomX continues to collaborate with major players like Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) (not held by the fund), fund holding Amgen (AMGN) and Moderna (MRNA) (not held) to advance its oncology pipeline.

  • Overall, the biotech industry experienced several positive developments that provided some counterbalance to the broader health care sector's ongoing 2025 challenges. Upside drivers included promising drug approvals and clinical progress (including gene therapies), a few announced strategic partnerships and investments, and signs of increased optimism about biotech IPOs (initial public offerings). Elsewhere in the portfolio, our high return in the health care services industry was due to a solitary overweight position in Guardant (GH) Health that rallied solidly. The fund's overall losses in the pharmaceuticals industry were partially mitigated by huge rallies for key contributor EyePoint Pharmaceuticals (EYPT) and a few others.

  • Conversely, the fund sustained a modest loss in the pharmaceuticals industry as off-benchmark or overweight positions in Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ), Contineum Therapeutics (CTNM) and Eli Lilly (LLY) fell much further than the benchmark average.

  • In the biotech industry, absolute and relative returns were hindered by declines for the fund's overweighted or off-benchmark investments in Benitec Biopharma (BNTC), Mineralys Therapeutics (MLYS), Arcutis Biotherapeutics (ARQT), Syndax Pharmaceuticals (SNDX), Zealand Pharma (OTCPK:ZLDPF), Day One Biopharmaceuticals (DAWN) and others that sustained 2Q25 losses of more than 10%. Relative returns were further pressured by an underweighting in Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) as its share price jumped nearly 21%, and by the portfolio's lack of exposure to Blueprint Medicines, Exelixis (EXEL) and other index component companies that rallied.

  • A minor portfolio allocation in the life sciences tools and services industry traded lower, with results versus the index hindered by a lack of exposure to Illumina (ILMN) and Tempus AI (TEM).

Outlook

  • The portfolio continues to emphasize mid-, small- and micro-capitalization biotech stocks, tilted toward what we believe to be clinically or commercially "derisked" assets.

  • Our positive outlook is based on what we consider to be generally strong fundamentals and appealing valuations backed partly by innovation in new drug modalities, a potentially strong merger-and-acquisition (M&A) cycle returning later this year (after sticking points related to tariffs and Most Favored Nation [MFN] pricing policies are resolved), and relatively stable Medicare drug reimbursement. As it pertains to M&A, we are still waiting to see implementation details in terms of how the Trump administration works through looming tariff and MFN pricing policy actions. We view these as important "clearing events" that can help ease investor uncertainty and potentially open up biotech M&A activity once they're clarified.

  • Conversely, we remain cognizant of risks to biopharma innovation and stock performance linked to the persistence of inflation and the potential for only minimal interest-rate reductions in the United States in the latter half of 2025. Recent developments related to US Food & Drug Administration staffing and global tariffs add to near-term uncertainty.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors' shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 9/1/2009. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 9/1/2009, a restated figure is used based on the fund's Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (B) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund's total annual operating expenses as of the fund's prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 08/31/2025 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. The portfolio is non-diversified and may invest in a relatively small number of issuers, which may negatively impact the fund's performance and result in greater fluctuation in the value of the fund's shares. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Glossary

An initial public offering (IPO) is the process of offering shares in a private corporation to the public for the first time.

The MSCI All Country World Index is a market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure equity market performance of developed and emerging markets. Source: MSCI makes no warranties and shall have no liability with respect to any MSCI data reproduced herein. No further redistribution or use is permitted. This report is not prepared or endorsed by MSCI.

data.title: Atlantic International's Position Is Risky, It Is Not An Opportunity
data.description:
Atlantic International (NASDAQ:ATLN) is a staffing company that provides temporary and permanent worker solutions to US customers.

The company is not growing and is not operationally profitable. In fact, it generates pretty large losses in accrual terms, albeit smaller in cash terms because of SBC.

In addition, the company is heavily indebted, with significant leverage and interest costs, which cannot be covered with operations.

This leads me to believe the company is too risky, not an opportunity, and therefore initiate coverage with a Hold.

Origins as SPAC

Atlantic International went public as a SPAC, called SeqLL, in 2021.

In late 2024, the company finally acquired part of an operating company by reverse merger, called Lyneer. SeqLL shareholders (which then changed its name to Atlantic International) owned about 53% of Lyneer back then. This process is described on page 5 of the company's FY24 10-K.

Lyneer is the operating business, a staffing company. The remaining portion is owned by a company called IDC, which owned Lyneer before the merger with SeqLL.

Low-margin staffing business

Atlantic states on its 10-K that it plans to operate as a platform to acquire staffing agency businesses. However, for the time being, the only relevant operating subsidiary in this business is Lyneer, so we will focus on it.

Lyneer's business is mostly that of a staffing agency, with almost 100 offices in the US and 300 full-time employees.

Lyneer offers companies the possibility to hire workers temporarily, and instead of incorporating them as its own workers (adding administrative and legal burdens), to pay Lyneer for their service. The workers show up as Lyneer's.

The company's revenues are shown as principal in this operation, because it pays the workers, and then charges the companies these workers work for, plus the company's margin. In a sense, though, the company's actual business is mostly the gross profit, which is much lower than revenues. For more information on how this accounting works, please review the Segment Reporting note of the company's latest 10-Q.

Unprofitable on a corporate basis

Atlantic does not report segment or subsidiary-specific numbers, in order to understand how profitable Lyneer's operations are, before corporate expenses.

We can only work with the aggregate figures, which show a pretty unprofitable company. The company's aggregate operating expenses, which include the SG&A necessary to operate Lyneer and corporate expenses, are almost 65% higher than the company's gross profits, and growing.

When reviewing this cost structure, it is hard to see how much of it is recurring.

The company's 10-K for FY24, which includes the first financials post-merger, mentioned that out of $65 million in SG&A for the year, versus $45 million the year before, the majority of the increase came from merger-related expenses.

I would have expected then, to see the expenses decrease in 1Q25. However, the 10-Q for the quarter shows that the company's OpEx continued to increase, even sequentially. SG&A was now $20 million, which, annualized reaches a level of $80 million. Again, the factor mentioned is merger-related costs.

We will need to see future financial results to observe a potential decrease in OpEx, very necessary to generate profitability. For the time being, however, Atlantic is operationally very unprofitable.

High leverage

Lyneer's acquisition carried with it significant debt, most of which is consolidated at the Atlantic level.

The majority of this debt, as explained under 'Liquidity & Capital Resources' on the company's 10-K, came about when Lyneer was acquired by IDC in 2021.

At least part of the acquisition was financed with different tranches of debt, plus a working capital revolver facility. This is not unusual, nor is the fact that Lyneer was jointly liable for the debt, as lenders want to have access to the operating business assets.

According to the 10-K, part of that debt was then assumed by IDC, and Lyneer was removed as a joint debtor. Further, the company has decided not to account for the part of the debt that IDC is expected to pay, even though that if IDC does not pay, Lyneer is still liable. This is what I make from the language in the 10-Q stating that 'The amounts recognized in the Company's financial statements represents its portion of amounts Lyneer expects to repay under its respective joint and several liability agreements as of March 31, 2025 and December 31, 2024, respectively. As of the date of the Merger, the Company deconsolidated its joint and several debt obligations as it believes it is reasonably probable that IDC has the ability to repay their portion.'

The above means that the company's balance sheet debt (which as we will see is already high) does not account for the totality of the potential liabilities to which the company is exposed, as a co-debtor with IDC. Only reflected on the balance sheets is already $67 million in debt. I believe additional potential liabilities are at least $55 million ($30 million from promissory notes, and another $25 million from working capital facility). This is based on reading Note 8: Debt on the 10-Q.

Of these, $35 million is owed to IDC (as part of the payment for the Merger in Merger Notes), and does not generate interest. It will become due when the company issues capital in the market, or in 2027, whichever happens first.

Another $28 million is a revolver facility jointly owned with IDC. In the subsequent events of the 10-Q, the company states that the revolver has been negotiated, and that the new lender has assumed the liabilities of IDC (not Lyneer's and therefore not Atlantic's), taking Atlantic shares as collateral. These shares are expected to be sold in the market.

Cash situation is not as bad but still very risky

When we look at the cash position of the company, it is a little better.

Part of it is due to stock-based compensation, which probably makes a big portion of the company's SG&A ($6 million in 1Q25 compared to $19 million in SG&A in the same quarter).

Still, if we added back these and other non-cash expenses (D&A, debt amortization mainly), the company was still down $2.5 million in cash from operations in 1Q25. In this quarter, the company posted a very positive CFO of $14 million entirely because of the reduction in working capital. However, this source of financing cannot last forever. The company used most of that cash to repay debt in the quarter.

Then, despite owing more than $60 million directly, the company's interest expenses in cash are lower at $1.5 million in 1Q25, or $6 million annualized.

This is because part of the debt ($35 million) is in Merger Notes to IDC, which does not carry interest. The interest on the revolving loan debt is not reported on the 10-Q (and the line has been renegotiated as mentioned).

We will need to see future financials to establish a more correct interest figure.

So far, outside of changes in working capital, the company is losing $4 million in cash per quarter (between operations and interest). As of 1Q25, the company had $1.5 million in cash and net working capital of about $18 million (without accounting for current debts).

That is, even assuming a continued decrease in working capital, the company's current balance sheet cannot sustain its current cash outflows for a lot longer.

Too risky for investment

It is basically not possible to value the stock of a company that generates no operational profitability, that has a stockholder's deficit of $15 million (even larger if we account for $30 million in intangible assets and whatever portion of the IDC debt for which Lyneer is jointly liable), and whose operations are not indicating any improvement in profitability.

The company's two reported quarters after the merger so far do not show growth in revenues or in margins meaningfully at the gross level. Further, SG&A continues to grow, annually and sequentially, and OpEx is already twice as high as gross profit.

This is a company that, even if growing, would take a long time to leverage the significant OpEx structure it has. And the company does not have the benefit of such a long time because its cash holdings are low, and it is currently cash negative on an operational basis (before changes in working capital).

For that reason, I believe the name is too risky for consideration and clearly not an opportunity. In future quarters, I would observe the dynamics in revenue growth, OpEx growth, and leverage with attention. Until then, I initiate Atlantic with a Hold rating.

Upside risks

My comments on the company are pretty negative, so why I don't rate it as a Sell? There are two reasons.

First, the company has only operated as a consolidated entity for two quarters, with significant difficulties in understanding the long-term financials. It could be, for example, that Lyneer's steady-state OpEx requirements are lower, and therefore the company has a path to profitability.

If that was the case, the company could post a materially better quarter (probably still unprofitable, but less so), which could lead, given its low market cap and even lower average trading volume, to a squeeze on the name. As shown below, the short interest on the name is almost five times its average daily volume, meaning it is prone to large upward movements with positive surprises or news.
